title: Ful nabed
subtitle: Egyptian white fava bean salad
excerpt: Dried white split fava beans cooked until just tender, then dressed while warm with spring onion, tomato, lemon, cumin, and olive oil. Served at room temperature as a salad or light breakfast dish. Lighter and fresher than the slow-cooked whole-bean ful medames — a different preparation entirely.
sourceNotes: Ful nabed (broad beans, peeled) uses dried split white fava beans (peeled and halved), which cook faster than whole favas and produce a lighter, less starchy result than ful medames. The preparation is closer to a warm salad than to the thick braised stew of ful medames. It is a distinct dish made from a different product.

recipe.batchNotes: Keeps refrigerated for 2 days. Dress with fresh lemon and olive oil when serving.

content.text: Dried split fava beans do not need soaking. Rinse and cook directly: they take 25 to 30 minutes at a gentle simmer and are done when they are tender but still holding their shape, with a slight bite. Do not overcook — they should be distinct in the salad, not dissolved.
Dress the beans while still warm so they absorb the lemon and olive oil. The spring onion and tomato go in just before serving, not while the beans are hot — the heat from the beans will wilt them.

Simmer the {{dried-split-fava-beans}} in plenty of salted water for 25 to 30 minutes until just tender but still holding their shape. Drain well. While still warm, toss with the {{garlic}}, {{cumin-ground}}, {{lemon-juice}}, {{olive-oil}}, and {{sea-salt-fine}}. Leave to cool to room temperature for 20 minutes. Stir in the {{spring-onion}}, {{tomato}}, and {{parsley-flat}}. Taste and adjust salt and lemon — the salad should be bright and assertively dressed. Serve with bread.

items.fix: Check from 20 minutes and pull off the heat as soon as each bean is tender throughout. Drain immediately into a sieve and do not leave in the cooking water

items.cause: Dressing applied too sparingly or not adjusted after cooling
items.fix: Taste again at room temperature. Cool salads need more acid and salt than when warm. Add another squeeze of lemon and a pinch of salt

items.cause: Beans overcooked until soft rather than salad-tender
items.fix: Ful nabed is a textured salad; bessara is a smooth dip. The beans must retain their structure. Stop cooking while there is still a slight bite

content.text: Ful in any form is the Egyptian breakfast. While ful medames uses whole dried brown favas cooked for hours into a thick stew, ful nabed uses the white split favas in a lighter, faster preparation that is closer to a salad. Both are eaten with bread, spring onion, tomato, and a drizzle of olive oil — the same accompaniments, different textures and intensities. This is a weekday breakfast dish: quick to cook, nutritious, and complete.
